column3: IN THE FIRST YEAR OF CYRUS KING OF THE PERSIANS] namely when Cyrus transferred the monarchy from the Chaldeans to the Persians. This was therefore the first year of Cyrus not of the Persian kingdom, i.e., when he himself was first made king and began to reign in Persia, from which point he reigned for thirty full years, as Eusebius and Africanus hold in their Chronicles, St. Jerome on Zach. ch. 10, Pererius on Daniel ch. 5, and others; but this was the first year of Cyrus's monarchy and of the Babylonian kingdom, namely when, having overcome Baltasar and captured and overthrown Babylon, he transferred the monarchy from the Chaldeans to himself and the Persians. For he could not have rescued the Jews from Babylon and from the hands of the Chaldeans unless he had overcome and conquered them. This is clearly evident from the words of Cyrus in v. 2.
[Genealogy of High Priests] Josedec, son of Saraiah, was transported to Babylon, where he served as High Priest during the entire period of captivity until Cyrus, 1 Chron. 6:15. # GEM
Joshua (or Jesus), son of Josedec, led the Jews back from Babylon to Jerusalem under Cyrus together with Zerubbabel, and rebuilt the Temple, Ezra 3:2.
Joakim, son of Joshua, held the High Priesthood under Darius Hystaspis and Xerxes, Neh. 12:10.
Eliashib, son of Joakim, was High Priest under Artaxerxes Longimanus, Neh. 3:1 and the last chapter vv. 4 and 6.
Joiada, son of Eliashib, was High Priest under Darius Nothus, son of Longimanus, Neh. 12:10.
Jonathan, son of Joiada, was High Priest under Artaxerxes Mnemon, son of Darius Nothus, Neh. 12:11.

Jaddua (or Jaddo), son of Jonathan, Neh. 12:11. He went out to meet Alexander the Great and reconciled him with the Jews when he was offended at them, as Josephus testifies, Antiq. bk. 11 at the end. Jaddo's brother was Manasseh, the first High Priest of the Samaritans on Mount Gerizim. See Josephus, Antiq. bk. 11, ch. 7.
Onias I, son of Jaddua, High Priest under Ptolemy Lagus, the first king of Egypt after Alexander, as Josephus, Eusebius and others testify.
Simon, son of Onias, surnamed the Just, whose praises Sirach describes in ch. 50.

Eleazar, brother of Simon and son of Onias. He sent 70 interpreters to Ptolemy Philadelphus, the second king of Egypt, who translated the sacred Scripture from Hebrew into Greek, as Josephus, Aristeas, Eusebius and others testify. # GEM

SYNOPSIS OF THE CHAPTER: Cyrus acknowledges the God of Israel, and therefore grants the Jews permission to emigrate from Babylon to Judea, and commands his prefects to assist them in rebuilding the city and Temple with his own help and resources; he restores the sacred vessels removed from the Temple by the Chaldeans. Therefore the Jews, rejoicing together with the Levites, return to their homeland.
THUS SAYS CYRUS KING OF THE PERSIANS] Cyrus here acknowledges God as the Lord of heaven and the giver of all kingdoms, and declares that he has been commanded by Him to build the Temple in Jerusalem.

Cyrus says "The Lord God of heaven has given me all the kingdoms of the earth," as noted in 3 Esdras 6:17, which states: "In the first year of the reign of Cyrus king of Babylon, king Cyrus wrote to build this house," etc. For although Darius the Mede, the uncle of Cyrus, is said to have succeeded to his kingdom after the capture of Babylon (Daniel 5, last verse), yet Darius, being old, yielded the entire government to his nephew Cyrus, a vigorous young man, and dying that same year left him the whole empire. Therefore Cyrus, in the very first year of his monarchy—which was the seventieth and final year of the captivity of the Jews in Babylon—freed them from that captivity.

Note from Herodotus, Justin, Eusebius, Josephus and others, that Cyrus began to reign over Persia alone in the 55th Olympiad, and from that point the thirty years of his universal reign are counted. He gradually extended his empire beyond Persia, and finally stormed Babylon and overthrew the Chaldean monarchy, making himself sole monarch. It was then that he dissolved the captivity of the Jews in Babylon, the seventy years of captivity prophesied by Jeremiah (chapters 25 and 29) having by then been completed. Cyrus did this in the 27th year of his Persian reign; and it is more probable that he reigned as monarch for only three more years after the capture of Babylon. This is what the Seder Olam, or Hebrew Chronicle, teaches, as do the Hebrews generally, and Lyra on Daniel, Vatablus on Daniel chapter 1, and others, as well as Josephus in Antiquities XI.2. Hence Daniel, briefly tracing the years of Cyrus through his deeds, does not go beyond the third year.

Nor does Xenophon in Book 8 of the Cyropaedia, where he assigns seven years to the principate of Cyrus, stand in the way. Wherefore Bellarmine, Torniellus, Salianus, and Ribera on Zechariah 1, n. 33, think Cyrus reigned seven years as monarch; because those books were written by Xenophon not so much for historical accuracy as to fashion in Cyrus the portrait of a true prince. Moreover, that seven-year principate of Cyrus could be reckoned from his subjugation not of Babylon but of Asia. Finally, more credence should be given to 3 Esdras 5 (last verse) than to Xenophon. For there it is stated quite explicitly that Cyrus reigned only two years from the beginning of the rebuilding of the temple, which, as is clear from this chapter, took place immediately upon the release from captivity, namely in the second year of the monarchy of Cyrus. For Ezra clearly teaches here in chapter 3, verse 8, that the Jews began to rebuild the temple in this second year; and in chapter 4, verse 6, he recounts that the construction of the temple was immediately hindered by the Cutheans for all the days of Cyrus. Josephus teaches the same more clearly in Book XI, chapter 2, where he says the Cutheans obstructed it without Cyrus knowing, since he was occupied with other affairs, and especially with the war against the Massagetae, in which he also perished. Hence in chapter 3, Ezra recounts that the construction of the temple, granted by Cyrus in the first year of his monarchy, was suspended for nine years: namely, for the two remaining years of Cyrus, and for the six during which his son Cambyses reigned, to whom Darius Hystaspis succeeded, who in the second year of his reign restored it. Cyrus therefore did not survive seven years, as some think. For how could Cyrus, so favorable to the Jews, have been ignorant for seven years of the obstacles placed by the Cutheans against the building of the temple, or have tolerated them? How could the Jews, and especially Daniel, not have informed Cyrus either personally or through ambassadors about the whole matter? Finally, Herodotus, Justin, Berosus and others write that after the capture of Babylon, Cyrus waged a final war against the Lydians who had revolted, and afterwards fell in battle against the Massagetae. All of these things Cyrus plausibly accomplished in three years, for he was most warlike and impatient of rest.

You ask: Whence did Cyrus receive his name, and what does it mean? First, Strabo in Book 15 thinks Cyrus received his name from the river Cyrus. Second, Vilalpando in Part 1, Apparatus, Book 3, chapter 2, page 137, considers that Cyrus was named from Cyno, that is, a dog, which is fabled to have nursed him, so that Cyrus is as it were κυνικός, that is, canine. For, as Herodotus recounts in the Clio, Astyages, the grandfather of Cyrus, handed the infant Cyrus over to Harpagus to expose and destroy him, because an oracle had told him that his kingdom would be taken from him by Cyrus. Harpagus handed him to Mitridates, a herdsman of Astyages, whose wife was called in Greek Cyno (κύων), meaning dog, and in Median, Spaco; for the Medes call a dog spaca. Accordingly Cyrus, as he grew up, would recall that he had been raised by the wife of the herdsman, always praising her, so that Cyno was ever on his lips. His parents, taking up this name so that it might seem divinely providential to the Persians that their son had been preserved, spread the story that Cyrus, when exposed, had been reared by a dog. Thus that legend arose. So says Herodotus. Likewise Trogus Pompeius hands down the story that Cyrus was raised by a dog. But Herodotus does not say that Cyrus was named from Cyno; and Cyrus appears to be a Persian name, not Greek. Wherefore Justin, Book 1, says: "The nurse afterwards had the name Sparco, because the Persians so call a dog" (for she had taken the child Cyrus, suckled by a dog in the woods, to raise as her own); "afterward, when he was a boy, he received the name Cyrus among the shepherds"—where Justin makes no mention of Cyno as the source of the name Cyrus.

Third, Suidas under the word Cyrus says: Cyrus in Greek is κύριος, meaning Lord, King; because in a game among youths he obtained the authority of king (which in Greek is κύριος). The Persians called Darius a tradesman, on account of the tributes he imposed; Cambyses a master; Cyrus a father. For Darius sold everything; Cambyses was fierce and reckless; but Cyrus was gentle and devoted to the welfare of his people. So says Suidas; but here again he assigns a Greek rather than a Persian etymology to Cyrus. Fourth, and more correctly, Plutarch in the Life of Artaxerxes Mnemon states: Cyrus in Persian is the same as the sun; for the Persians revered the sun as a divinity, just as they revered Cyrus their monarch; hence Jeremiah calls Cyrus the liberator, as it were the sun, of the Jews.

Why Cyrus, or a king, should be compared to the sun I showed aptly through eighteen analogies on Isaiah 41, verse 1. It is said that Cyrus saw in a dream the sun standing at his feet, and when he tried to grasp it the sun fled away, and this happened three times; when the Magi were asked what this portended, they replied that it portended Cyrus would reign for thirty years. Cyrus therefore shone among the kings of the nations and among the Persians like the sun, alike in wisdom, justice, temperance, magnanimity, and liberality, and was thus an example of all royal virtues, as Xenophon says. Hear Cicero in Book 1 On Divination, quoting Dionysius the Persian: "When the sun appeared to him (Cyrus) as he slept at his feet, he writes that he three times tried vainly to lay hands on it as the sun, revolving upon itself, slipped away and departed; and the Magi (who were held to be a class of wise and learned men among the Persians) told him that the threefold attempt upon the sun portended that Cyrus would reign thirty years—which so came to pass, for he reached his seventieth year, having begun to reign at the age of forty." # GEM

Furthermore, Cyrus in Hebrew is the same as one who is like his own Christ, promising to submit nations to him and to make him divine and powerful. Hear Isaiah: "Who says to Cyrus: He is my shepherd, and he shall perform all my pleasure; who says to Jerusalem: You shall be built, and to the temple: Your foundation shall be laid." And again in chapter 45, verse 1: "Thus says the Lord to his anointed, to Cyrus, whose right hand I have held, to subdue nations before him, and to loose the loins of kings."

WHO IS THERE AMONG YOU OF ALL HIS PEOPLE? (that is, who desires to return from Babylon to Jerusalem?) HIS GOD BE WITH HIM, AND LET HIM GO UP TO JERUSALEM, AND BUILD THE HOUSE OF THE LORD GOD. Whoever among the Jews or proselytes in my kingdom desires to return to their Judaea, let them gather together as one, so that they may return together from captivity to their homeland: for I give them full liberty to return, and to rebuild the city and the temple; indeed I pray for the favour and help of their God to accomplish this. For HE IS THE GOD WHO IS IN JERUSALEM; Vatablus: who is that true and only God: as if to say, There is no other true God than the God whom the Israelites have worshipped and do worship in Jerusalem. Cyrus therefore here professes that he acknowledges the God of the Jews to be the true God, and that the other Gods of the Gentiles are not true gods, or at least are of lesser dignity and power, and subject to the God of the Hebrews, whom Plato and the philosophers called demigods. Hence Xenophon in book 8 of the Cyropaedia asserts that Cyrus also worshipped the gods of the nations, and when about to die had a dream in which he was invited to ascend to the gods; wherefore he adored and invoked Jupiter and the Sun, and spoke much about the immortality of the soul, that after death he would pass to the gods, and so died.

AND ALL THE REST IN EVERY PLACE WHERE HE DWELLETH, LET THE MEN OF HIS PLACE HELP HIM WITH SILVER AND GOLD: that is, the remaining Jews who have fixed their abode in Babylon or other places, have taken wives, begotten children, bought fields, so that they cannot or will not leave these things and return to Jerusalem — let these assist those who desire to return with silver and gold, both for the provisions of so long a journey, and for the restoration of the city and temple.

MEN OF HIS PLACE, that is, let each man in his place and from his place, namely from his fields and houses, supply what he has. Cyrus therefore here constitutes the Jews remaining in Babylon as a kind of tributary body toward those returning to Jerusalem. Furthermore, all the rest — not only Jews but also other peoples subject to my dominion — shall likewise assist those returning to Jerusalem, especially the prefects of the provinces, whom Josephus calls the king's friends. Hence these, by this edict of Cyrus, assisted the Jews in their return.

Excepto quod voluntarie offerunt templo Dei — The word "excepto" is a word of augmenting and confirming: as if to say, I know that many have already mentally designated and resolved in their minds certain gifts which they themselves wish to offer to the temple; those therefore I do not forbid, but praise and confirm, and I desire them to be offered; but beyond this I wish each person moreover to add other things, which may serve as provisions for those returning on their journey, and for the rebuilding of the city and houses, and for other manifold necessities and uses. Hence some think that Cyrus assessed a sum which each person was obliged to contribute to them, as Darius and Artaxerxes, successors of Cyrus, afterwards did; indeed that Cyrus decreed that the temple should be built from the royal treasury and at his own expense, as is clear from c. 6, v. 4. Hence instead of "excepto quod voluntarie offerunt," the Hebrew has "cum muneribus spontaneis": as if to say, Along with the offering which they freely bring to the temple, they shall also give other things for provisions, etc. So Vatablus, Pagninus and others.

Et surrexerunt principes patrum de Juda et Benjamin — "Principes patrum" are the heads of families; for each family had its own head and father: but different families descending from one head had their own prince and quasi-Patriarch.

Rex quoque Cyrus protulit vasa templi Domini, quae tulerat Nabuchodonosor — These were 5,400 in number, as is said in v. 11; all of which, sacrilegiously taken by the Chaldeans and dedicated to their idol Bel, Cyrus restored to God and to the temple.

Per manum Mithridatis filii Gazabar — The word "filii" is not in the Hebrew nor in the Greek. Hence Vatablus, Pagninus, Marinus, Cajetan and others translate: "By the hand of Mithridates the treasurer"; Josephus: "By Mithridates, prefect of the royal treasury." For "Agaza" is called "gazabar," that is, treasurer, as is clear from c. 7:21, where our translator renders it: "Custodes arcae." Unless you say that our translator understood the word "ben," that is, "son," to be implied, and that Gazabar was a common name but proper to an office, just as we commonly call the one who presides over a prince's treasury the treasurer, as though this name belonged to him not from baptism but from office proper, in which sons often succeed parents. Furthermore, Mithridates is compounded from "Mithri," that is, "major," "dynast," "prince," and "dath," that is, "of the law": as if to say, a dynast bearing the law and pronouncing judgment.

Et annumeravit eas Sassabasar principi Judae — That is, to Zerubbabel, grandson of King Jeconiah through his father Salathiel. So Josephus, Lyra, Dionysius, Vatablus, Mariana, Tornielus, Salianus and others. Wherefore both the father Salathiel and the grandfather Jeconiah seem to have died in this first year of Cyrus: for thereafter there is no mention of them, but always of Zerubbabel, as leader of Judah. Furthermore, Salathiel gave his newborn son the name Zerubbabel, that is, "stranger in Babylon," because he begot him in the Babylonian captivity; but when Evil-merodach brought his grandfather Jeconiah out of prison and made him a guest at his royal table, the name of Zerubbabel was changed so that he was called Sassabasar, that is, "rejoicing in tribulation." So Eusebius, lib. II, De Praeparatione, c. 3 and 4; for "sas" means "rejoicing," "ba" signifies "in," "shar" signifies "tribulation." Again, Pagninus in his Hebrew Names interprets Sassabasar as "six in tribulation"; for "ses" signifies "six," or "byssus"; the Hebrews indeed relate that he was so called because he stood firm and constant in six tribulations, so that there was fulfilled in him that word of Job 5:19: "In six tribulations he will deliver thee, and in the seventh evil shall not touch thee." # GEM

Note here that it was formerly customary among Hebrews, Chaldeans, Persians, Phoenicians and other Oriental peoples to change the names of foreigners and to give them names native and proper to their own nation into which they were enrolled; native names, I say, signifying something definite and suited to the person, just as Adam did in giving things names suited to their nature, Genesis 2:19, so that names might be as it were omens signifying some present or future nature, virtue, or property of the thing. Thus Daniel c. 1, the Hebrew names Ananias, Azarias, and Misael were changed in Babylon by the Chaldeans into the Chaldean names Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego. Thus c. 2:63, the Hebrew Nehemiah was called by the Chaldeans in Chaldean "Athersatha." The same concerning the Greeks is taught by Plato in the Cratylus. Wherefore Eusebius, lib. II, De Praeparatione, c. 3 and 4, draws from this an argument for the exquisite wisdom of the Hebrews, namely that sacred names...
The Nethinim (v.43ff) were temple servants given by Joshua; they were Gibeonites (and therefore Canaanites, not Jews) who were condemned to this service on account of their deceit. See Joshua 9.

Just as Joshua gave the Gibeonites, so Solomon gave others—captured in war or proselytes—to the temple. These are called "servants of Solomon"; many of them were assigned by him to the construction of the temple, 2 Chron. 2:17.
Telmela, Tel-harsha, Cherub, Addan, and Immer are names of Chaldean cities from which many emigrated to Judea. These could not prove that they were descended from Jews, as follows.

The sons of Barzillai, who (Barzillai) took a wife from the daughters of Barzillai the Gileadite (a priest), and was called by their name (i.e. of the priests). That is to say: this Barzillai married a daughter of Barzillai the priest, and through her was called a priest, i.e. sprung from priestly stock; but since this could not be proved from the public registers of genealogies, both he and his sons were excluded from the priesthood until God should be consulted.

And Atersatha said: this is Nehemiah, say Lyra, Cajetan, and Vatablus, as is clear from Neh. 7:65, where the same thing is narrated. Hence this history seems to have occurred later under Nehemiah; and Neh. 8:9 says "And Nehemiah (he is the Atersatha)"—that is, the king's cupbearer, so called by the Chaldeans from the Chaldean verb tersa, meaning he nourished, and scata, meaning he drank, i.e. one who nourishes with drink, that is, the king's cupbearer. Nehemiah here speaks of himself in the third person, as also in ch. 12:26; but in the book of Nehemiah he always speaks of himself in the first person. Hence some have thought that the Nehemiah who was Ezra's companion is a different person from the Nehemiah who appears in 2 Esdras and is its author. On Atersatha I shall speak again at Neh. 8:9.

That they should not eat of the most holy things: that is, of the victims consecrated to God—offered and sacrificed—meaning they should not eat of the sacrifices offered for sin, nor of the right shoulder from the peace offerings; for these fell to priests alone, and these men could not prove themselves to be priests.

Until there stood up a priest with Urim and Thummim: that is, until some high priest, vested in the pontifical garments in which the Urim and Thummim were set, consulting God, should be perfectly instructed by him whether these men were of priestly descent or not. It is therefore a metonymy. Note: the Urim and Thummim were inscribed or woven into the Rational (breastplate) of the high priest. Urim in Hebrew means illuminations; Thummim, perfections—because the high priest, clothed in pontifical vestments, is perfectly illuminated by God concerning those things about which he consulted him, as is clear from Num. 27:21. Allegorically, Christ the High Priest was signified, through whom light and truth were made, John 1. Tropologically, by these two both the pontiff and the people were admonished to pursue truth of doctrine and perfection of life; and anagogically, to aspire through these two to the light of glory and the perfect enjoyment and love of God. # GEM

All the congregation together was 42,360. This is the sum of those who returned to Judea with Jeshua and Zerubbabel; yet you will not find it if you add up the numbers of the individual families assigned here, for you will find only 29,818. To complete the total given here, 12,542 must be added—these being those who returned to Jerusalem from the ten tribes together with the tribes of Judah and Benjamin, but are not enumerated here. Moreover these same persons are numbered in nearly the same way, with a few exceptions, in Neh. 7:6.

And of them two hundred singing men and singing women. Male singers were employed in the temple for the chanting of Psalms; female singers for secular songs, e.g. at funerals, weddings, banquets, etc. This signifies how very joyful the return of the Hebrews from Babylon was—that they sang and shouted for joy, according to the oracle of Isaiah 66: "They shall bring all your brethren from all nations as an offering to the Lord on horses, and in chariots, and in litters, and on mules, and in wagons, to my holy mountain Jerusalem." And Psalm 126: "When the Lord turned again the captivity of Zion, we were like those who are comforted" (Heb. like those who dream—that is, so exulting that they seemed to dream of returning, rather than truly returning to their homeland). "Then was our mouth filled with laughter, and our tongue with singing."
Their horses, 736: so the Hebrew and the Roman (Vulgate). Some wrongly read "six hundred."

When they came to the temple of the Lord: the temple had already been burned by the Chaldeans. "Temple" here therefore means the place where the temple had stood, because that place was sacred and appointed by God for sacrifices and divine worship. Hence the Jews soon erected an altar there and sacrificed upon it, as is clear from Jer. 41 and Bar. 1:10.

Sixty-one thousand drams of gold: that is, sixty-one thousand; so read the Hebrew and the Roman (Vulgate). Others read "forty thousand" instead of "sixty thousand," for that number is given at Neh. 7:70, on which more there. This divergence seems to have arisen from an easy and common transposition of numerals: for lx is 60, and xl is 40. See Francis Lucas discussing this matter at length in his notes.

The seventh month had now come — called Tishri in Hebrew, corresponding partly to our September and partly to October. The Hebrews used lunar months, each determined by a single new moon. From this it appears that the Jews departed from Babylon at the beginning of spring, in the first year of Cyrus, and after four months of travel arrived in Jerusalem in the month of July, and straightway erected the altar and prepared themselves for the Feast of Tabernacles and the other celebrations appointed for the seventh month. In like manner Ezra, in the seventh year of Artaxerxes, departed from Babylon on the first day of the first month and arrived in Jerusalem on the first day of the fifth month, as we shall hear in chapter seven, verse one.

And Jeshua (or Jesus), son of Jozadak — who upon the death of his father Jozadak had succeeded him in the High Priesthood, and was the nephew of Ezra through a brother, for Ezra was the brother of Jozadak — and Zerubbabel son of Shealtiel, grandson of Jeconiah, who upon the death of his grandfather and father had succeeded to the leadership of the people. These two therefore were the leaders of the people returning to Jerusalem: Jeshua in ecclesiastical matters, Zerubbabel in political affairs.

They celebrated the solemnity — that is, they kept the Feast of Tabernacles, or Scenopegia, on the fifteenth day of the seventh month for seven days — and offered holocausts day by day in order according to the commandment, the work of each day in its day; that is, they offered each day throughout the entire octave of the feast as many victims, of such kinds and by such rite, as God had commanded to be offered in Numbers 29.
And after these things the continual holocaust — concerning which I spoke at Numbers 28:3.

In the second year of their coming to the temple — that is, to the place where the temple had stood, already burned by the Chaldeans — of God in Jerusalem, in the second month, they began to rebuild the temple.

When therefore the temple had been founded by the masons — that is, when the foundations of the temple had now been laid — that they might praise God through the hands of David, that is, through the works of David, namely through the Psalms composed by David.
The enemies of Judah and Benjamin heard that the children of the captivity were building a temple to the Lord God of Israel.

They approached Zerubbabel and the chief fathers, saying: Let us build with you, for we seek your God as you do; behold, we have sacrificed to him since the days of Esar-haddon king of Assyria, who brought us here.

Zerubbabel, Joshua, and the rest of the chief fathers of Israel said to them: It is not for you and us to build a house to our God, but we ourselves alone will build to the Lord God of Israel, as Cyrus king of Persia has commanded us.
So the people of the land weakened the hands of the people of Judah and troubled them in building.
They hired counselors against them to frustrate their purpose all the days of Cyrus king of Persia, even until the reign of Darius king of Persia.
In the reign of Ahasuerus, at the beginning of his reign, they wrote an accusation against the inhabitants of Judah and Jerusalem.

And in the days of Artaxerxes, Bishlam, Mithredath, Tabeel, and the rest of their companions wrote to Artaxerxes king of Persia; the letter of accusation was written in Aramaic and translated in the Aramaic tongue.
Rehum the commander and Shimshai the scribe wrote one letter against Jerusalem to king Artaxerxes, as follows:

Rehum the commander and Shimshai the scribe, and the rest of their companions, the Dinaites, the Apharsathchites, the Tarpelites, the Apharsites, the Archevites, the Babylonians, the Susanchites, the Dehavites, and the Elamites,
and the rest of the nations whom the great and noble Osnapper deported and settled in the cities of Samaria and in the rest of the region beyond the River, and now:
This is the copy of the letter they sent to him. To Artaxerxes the king: your servants, the men of the region beyond the River, send greetings.
Be it known to the king that the Jews who came up from you to us have gone to Jerusalem, that rebellious and wicked city, which they are building, restoring its walls and joining the foundations.
Now be it known to the king that if this city is rebuilt and its walls restored, they will not pay tribute, custom, or toll, and the royal revenue will be damaged.
Now because we eat the salt of the palace and it is not proper for us to see the king dishonored, therefore we send and inform the king,

that search may be made in the book of the records of your fathers: you will find in the records and know that this city is a rebellious city, harmful to kings and provinces, and that sedition has been stirred up in it from of old; that is why this city was destroyed.
We inform the king that if this city is rebuilt and its walls restored, you will then have no possession in the province beyond the River.

# GEM Lapide notes that Zerubbabel wisely refused the Samaritans' offer, recognizing that their worship was corrupted and mixed with idolatry since the days of Esar-haddon, and that to admit them as partners would be to contaminate the sacred work with impure associates, following the principle that the holy must not be joined with the profane.

Lapide observes that the enemies, having failed to infiltrate by guile, now resorted to open opposition and hired counselors at court to obstruct the work, demonstrating that when the devil cannot corrupt the Church from within by heresy he attacks it from without by persecution.

The opposition lasted through the entire reign of Cyrus until Darius, showing that the Church's trials are prolonged and that patience is required of the faithful, who must persevere despite continuous adversarial pressure from worldly powers.

Lapide identifies this Ahasuerus with Cambyses, son and successor of Cyrus, noting that chronological difficulties arise here and that various interpreters differ, but that the accusation was lodged at the very beginning of his reign, indicating the enemies' eagerness to exploit any change of ruler.

The letter was written in Aramaic (Syriace), that is, in the Chaldean or Syrian tongue, which was then the common diplomatic language of the Persian empire, just as Latin was later the common tongue of the Western Church and of learned correspondence.

Lapide notes the calumny: the enemies call Jerusalem a rebellious and wicked city, twisting its ancient history of resistance against Babylonian and Assyrian kings—resistance which was in fact just and holy—into a pretext for accusing the Jews of sedition against Persia.

The argument about tribute is a calculated appeal to royal avarice, a device Lapide sees as typically diabolical: when theological or moral arguments fail against God's work, opponents resort to stirring up the cupidity of those in power.

'We eat the salt of the palace': Lapide explains this as a proverbial expression for those who live on royal bounty and are therefore bound to the king's interests, paralleling the Roman phrase about those who eat another's bread and must serve his will.

Hosts of Judah: namely the Samaritans, or inhabitants of Samaria, enemies of the Jews, as is clear from vv. 2 and 10. On these see 2 Kings 17:24. Sons of the captivity: that is, the Jews recently captive, and now returned from captivity.
Asor Haddan: this is Esar-haddon, son of Sennacherib, last king of the Assyrians, of whom see 2 Kings 19, final verse.

There is no fellowship between you and us to build a house to our God: because you are schismatics, indeed heretics and idolaters; for together with our God and true worship you venerate idols which you brought from Assyria, from which you were transplanted into Samaria. The Jews therefore refused to have the Samaritans as partners in the building. First, because they were heretics: hence learn what communion with heretics we ought to shun, especially in sacred matters and sacraments. Second, because they were enemies of the Jews, and might have committed fraud in the building, constructing an idol instead of a temple. Third, lest the Samaritans should claim the temple as if built by themselves, or at least seek to possess a portion of it, and therein celebrate their superstitions and idol-worship. Fourth, lest by evil craft they should mix sand for lime and earth for stones in the building, so that the structure of the temple should become ruinous and at last collapse.
People of the land: the aforementioned Samaritans, settlers and inhabitants of the neighboring land, namely Samaria.

They hired counselors: that is, counselors, namely the Persian prefects and tax-collectors in Samaria, Syria, and Judea, so that while Cyrus lived, knowing him to favor the building of the city and temple, they would supply funds slowly and sparingly, and by other tricks and evil arts impede the work. But after Cyrus died in the following year, they approached his son Cambyses and wrote him letters accusing the Jews as disturbers of the peace and seditious men; and thus obtained a rescript from him by which the Jews were commanded to cease from the building. Note here that the letters of both kings and prefects in vv. 6 and 7 are written in the Hebrew text in their own idiom, namely Chaldean, not Hebrew. The same occurs in Daniel. Especially because the Hebrews, recently captive in Babylon, had learned the Chaldean language there, and on returning thence fell into it, mixing the ancestral Hebrew tongue with Chaldean: from which mixture arose the Syro-Hebrew or Chaldeo-Hebrew language, which others call Jerusalemite. Thus the Latin language, mixed with Gothic by the Goths, produced for us the Spanish, Italian, and French languages.

One may ask: Why did the Jews not have recourse to Cyrus who favored them, and expose to him the frauds and tricks of the Samaritans and prefects? Answer: Because, as Josephus testifies, Cyrus had left home, gone to war against the revolting Lydians, and then against the Scythians, where he also perished in the following year. With Cyrus absent, Cambyses governed at home; whose counselors the Samaritans corrupted with gifts to impede the building of the temple. This lasted for two years, as the book of 3 Esdras, c. 5, last verse, records; from which it appears that the eight years commonly assigned to the reign of Cambyses must be reckoned from the time when, while his father Cyrus was occupied among the Lydians and Massagetae, he himself presided over the affairs of the empire at home. For after two years, Cyrus being slain, Cambyses reigned alone for only six years. See Josephus.

In the reign of Ahasuerus: that is, of Cambyses. So Josephus, and it is clear from the preceding, for he succeeded his father Cyrus in the kingdom. Note: Ahasuerus in Chaldean, Artaxerxes in Persian, was the common name of the kings of Persia and Babylon, just as the common name of the kings of Egypt was Pharaoh, and afterwards Ptolemy, and of the Romans Caesar. That this is so is clear from this chapter and from Esther 1:1 compared with c. 13:1 and c. 16:1. Ahasuerus, or as it is said in Chaldean, Achasueros, is derived from achias, meaning great, and ros, meaning head, that is, great head, namely a great king and prince. Hence with nearly the same letters transferred into another language, but with the vowels changed, in Greek it is called Oxyares, meaning sharp Mars, martial, warlike; and Xerxes, which word in Greek, says Herodotus bk. 6, means warrior; and Artaxerxes, great warrior. Hence Scaliger, sect. 6, p. 2, and other experts in languages assert that these names are nearly the same and mean the same thing, namely Oxyares, Ochlosarces, Ochsoarses, Xerxes, Ahasuerus, Arxaxes, Artaxerxes, or as the Persians pronounce it Xextes, Artaxextes, or Artaxata: for so it stands in the Hebrew, v. 3.

In the days of Artaxerxes: this is the same Cambyses, who in Chaldean is called Ahasuerus, in Persian Artaxerxes, as I have already said from the Septuagint. Hence the word Artaxerxes is not in the Hebrew. That this is so is clear from the passage.
The king sent a word to Rehum Beltethmus and Shimshai the scribe and to the rest who were in their council, the inhabitants of Samaria, and to the others beyond the River, greeting and peace.
The accusation which you sent to us has been plainly read before me.
And a command was issued by me, and they searched and found that that city has from ancient days rebelled against kings, and seditions and wars have been stirred up in it.
For mighty kings have been in Jerusalem, who also ruled over all the region that is beyond the River, and tribute, toll, and revenue were paid to them.
Now therefore give the command: that those men be stopped, that that city be not built, until a command is issued by me.
Take heed that you be not negligent in this, lest the evil grow little by little against the kings.

Then the copy of the edict of King Artaxerxes was read before Rehum Beltethmus and Shimshai the scribe and their counselors; and they went in haste to Jerusalem to the Jews and stopped them by arm and force.
Then the work of the house of the Lord in Jerusalem was interrupted and was not carried on until the second year of the reign of Darius king of the Persians.

On the reading of the Septuagint: those who here are said to have written to Ahasuerus are there read as having written to Artaxerxes. Indeed, some codices here at v.6 read thus: "In the reign moreover of Ahasuerus, he is Artaxerxes" (so the Complutensian; and this is clear from the end of the chapter, down to Darius); for Cambyses alone intervened as legitimate king between Cyrus and Darius. The same is clear from Josephus. For Ezra, about to describe the manner in which the prefects wrote to the king, says Cajetan, resumes according to Hebrew custom the time at which they wrote, naming the same person by another name, namely the Persian name, which he was accustomed to place at the head of his letters. For Ezra seems to have transcribed the letter of the Samaritans from the public chronicles and diaries of the Persians along with its heading, which was: "And in the days of Artaxerxes," so that whom Ezra calls Ahasuerus, the Persian chronicles called by the Persian name Artaxerxes. Otherwise Christoph. a Castro on Haggai ch.1 near the beginning, who thinks Ahasuerus is Cambyses, and Artaxerxes is the great Smerdis, who succeeded Cambyses and thrust himself into the kingdom by fraud. Worse, Scaliger thinks Ahasuerus is Xerxes, and Artaxerxes is Longimanus the son of Xerxes, of whom more below. A similar resumption of speech occurs at Mark 15:8-9 and frequently elsewhere.

Cambyses was an enemy of the Jews, hence he was called by them the second Nebuchadnezzar, as Eusebius testifies in his Chronicle. For Cambyses was a contemner of the gods, a sacrilegious man and a parricide: for he killed his own brother, says Justin, and took his sister to wife, whom he afterward slew. Wherefore by just divine vengeance he killed himself; for when mounting his horse the scabbard of his sword slipped off, and the naked blade was driven into his thigh, of which wound he died shortly after, says Herodotus and Justin.

Cajetan notes that although the Samaritans under Cyrus had impeded through counselors the building of the temple from public and royal funds, the Jews nevertheless did not cease from the work, but continued it from private and their own funds; wherefore they also impeded this by letters sent to Cambyses.

REHUM.] This is the name of an office signifying prefect of accounts, or master of wisdom, says Lyran. But Arias insists it is a proper name, signifying possessor of taste, or of discernment, or of reason, or of eloquence. For the Hebrew and Chaldean word te'em means flavor, taste, and metaphorically wisdom; the Hebrew Baal and Chaldean beel means Lord, prefect, president; therefore Beelteem, or as the Hebrews pronounce it Baaltaam, is equivalent to president of reason and counsel, which was exercised in the Senate and council, that is, Chancellor. For it was his office to report all matters to the king, to record them in writing on the public tablets, and to seal them with his hand and seal. So Josephus.
ASNAPPER.] This is Esarhaddon, or Sennacherib, or some other from his kindred or relations.

IF THE WALLS (of the city of Jerusalem, which the Jews are building) ARE RESTORED, TRIBUTE, etc. THEY WILL NOT PAY,] because trusting in their walls they will rebel and assert their liberty (of which they are most desirous). Hence it is clear that the Jews, though freed from Babylon, nevertheless remained tributaries and taxpayers to Cyrus and the Persians. And further, that Cyrus gave the Jews permission to rebuild not only the temple but also the city.

NOW WE, BEING MINDFUL OF THE SALT WHICH WE EAT IN THE PALACE,] that is to say, we mindful of the sustenance and salary (for this is derived from salt) which you have given us in court, so as to show a grateful spirit toward you our sustainer, we warn you of the danger which threatens you from the building of the city of Jerusalem, that you may impede it. Thus Pythagoras with this symbol: "Pass not by salt and a table," admonished his followers of gratitude, as if to say: In prosperous circumstances let not the gratitude owed to friends who made you a partaker of their salt and table slip from memory. For salt denotes a banquet, because in it all things are seasoned with salt to give them savor. The Hebrew is: Because we are salted with the salt of the palace. So the Septuagint, that is, because we were nourished in your palace. It can also be read: We have salted the temple with salt, that is, we have utterly destroyed it; for in former times when they wished to desolate a place they would sow salt in it.
THEN THE WORK OF THE HOUSE OF GOD (the building of the temple) IN JERUSALEM WAS INTERRUPTED, AND IT WAS NOT CONTINUED UNTIL THE SECOND YEAR OF THE REIGN OF DARIUS KING OF PERSIA.

On the question: Who was this Darius? Scaliger and Severus Sulpicius, Book 2 of his History, think he is Darius Nothus, who was the illegitimate or spurious son of Artaxerxes Longimanus, and reigned after him for 19 years. For Scaliger contends that Ahasuerus, mentioned at v.6, is Xerxes; that Artaxerxes of v.7 is Longimanus the son of Xerxes; that the Darius of this verse is Nothus; that Artaxerxes who in ch.7 sent Ezra and Nehemiah to Jerusalem is Artaxerxes surnamed Mnemon, the son and successor of Darius Nothus. Hence, says he, the temple was built under Darius Nothus, the city of Jerusalem under Artaxerxes Mnemon, and the 70 weeks of Daniel ch.9, which extended to the coming of Christ, are to be reckoned from the second year of Darius Nothus. So Scaliger, who, while he alone plays the sharpest Aristarchus of all chronologers, in turn provokes all to be sharper Aristarchuses against himself. # GEM For that the temple and city were built not under Darius Nothus but under the son of Hystaspes, who succeeded Cambyses, is clear from the common consent of chronologers, interpreters, and doctors. Second, from Josephus, who teaches this expressly. Third, from 3 Esdras ch.4, where this Darius vowed to build the temple if he should become king, of which more presently. Fourth, because according to Scaliger from Cyrus and from the ending of the Babylonian captivity to the restoration of the walls of Jerusalem there would have elapsed 144 years; for so many pass from Cyrus to Darius Nothus: which though Scaliger admits, it contradicts—

Those who had been led captive from Jerusalem to Babylon, while still surviving, seeing the new construction of the new temple of Zerubbabel, regarded it as of little or no worth compared to the former temple of Solomon. Now from the burning of Solomon's temple by the Chaldeans up to Darius Nothus, 176 years elapsed, as Scaliger himself says. Who, I ask, in that age prolonged his life to the year 176? Again, if Ezra was sent by Artaxerxes Mnemon to Jerusalem, then Ezra lived nearly two hundred years: for he was born of Seraiah the High Priest, who was slain by the Chaldeans in the first year of the Babylonian captivity. Moreover, Zerubbabel and Jeshua the son of Jozadak, who under Cyrus presided over the return from captivity, and afterward were present at this rebuilding of the temple under Darius, would have been 130 or 140 years old, when nevertheless in 3 Esdras ch. 3, v. 6, Zerubbabel is called a young man, one of the bodyguards of Darius. This Darius therefore was not Nothus, but Hystaspes. So says Josephus, book 11 of the Antiquities. # GEM

[Preamble] Nicol., Briani, Genebr., Torniellius, Salianus, and others generally among the more recent scholars. I shall resolve the arguments of Scaliger in Nehemiah, ch. 12:10 and ch. 13:28; he wishes that Sanballat himself, of whom the discussion there takes place, was a contemporary of the high priest Jaddua and of Alexander the Great, but this is false. Therefore in the second year of Darius Hystaspes the construction of the temple was resumed, having been interrupted for nine years, and in the sixth year of the same it was completed—namely, the construction of the Holy Place and the Holy of Holies; for the court, or outer part of the temple, and consequently the whole temple as regards all things, was finished in the sixth year of Darius, as Josephus relates, bk. 11, ch. 4. Now the cause which moved Darius to rebuild the temple was Zerubbabel, who was a most welcome courtier of Darius and held in special favour; and while Darius was still a prince, Zerubbabel had suggested to him that he make a vow concerning the ordering of the temple if he should be made king. When he was made king, the question having been proposed—What is the strongest of all things?—when one had said it was wine, another that it was women, and the third that it was the king, Zerubbabel said it was truth, and he discoursed on this matter so wisely and eloquently that Darius commanded him to ask whatever he wished. Upon hearing this, Zerubbabel began thus, 3 Esdras ch. 4:43: *Remember thy vow which thou didst vow to build Jerusalem on the day thou didst take the kingdom, and to restore all the vessels that were taken out of Jerusalem, which Cyrus set apart when he vowed to destroy Babylon, and vowed to send them back thither. And thou didst vow to build up the temple which the Edomites burned when Judaea was made desolate by the Chaldeans. And now this is that I demand and desire of thee, O lord the king: this is the royal munificence which I desire of thee, that thou fulfil the vow which thou with thine own mouth didst vow to the King of heaven.* Then Darius the king arose and kissed him, and wrote letters to all the treasurers and governors and captains, that they should escort him and all those who should go up with him.
[THE GREAT KING OF ISRAEL] Great in wisdom, resources, dominion, fame and glory, namely Solomon.

[TAKE THESE VESSELS, AND GO, AND PLACE THEM IN THE TEMPLE WHICH IS IN JERUSALEM, AND LET THE HOUSE OF GOD BE BUILT] This is a hysteron proteron, or inverted order: for the house of God, i.e. the temple, had first to be built before the vessels carried off by the Chaldeans could be placed in it. So Cajetan. Or certainly by "temple" understand the place and the treasury-chamber adjacent to the temple already burned down: for in this place the vessels and gifts of the temple were stored and kept.

[IN THE LIBRARY OF THE KING] Chaldean: bebet qidnzaia, that is, as the Septuagint renders it, in the house of the treasures of the royal treasury, where there was a library not of philosophers but of books, and an archive of writings pertaining chiefly to wealth, expenditures (e.g. those which Cyrus had made for the temple, ch. 6:8), census records, and monetary affairs. So Vatablus. Likewise there were kept in it the authentic copies of royal laws and decrees.

[IN ECBATANA] Hebrew: in Achmata; for the letters beth and mem are labials, and therefore are often interchanged, one being substituted for the other. [IN MEDIA] Hebrew: Madai, that is, Media, or the kingdom of the Medes, which had now been subjugated by the Persians and reduced to a province.

[THE HEIGHT OF SIXTY CUBITS] The former temple of Solomon was twice as high, for it was 120 cubits high; but Cyrus did not wish this new one to be built so high, lest the Jews should make it a citadel from which to rebel.

[AND THE WIDTH OF SIXTY CUBITS] By width here understand extension, whether it be in length or in breadth. So the Chaldean: for it forbade the old temple to extend in any direction beyond 60 cubits. Hence Lyran. says: "width," that is, length; for the length of the temple, i.e. of the Holy Place and the Holy of Holies, was 60 cubits; but the width was twenty. So Ribera, in his first book on the temple.

[Commentary on the building of the temple] Darius narrates that the vessels have already been restored, in accordance with the edict of Cyrus, which Cyrus had previously ordered to be replaced in the temple — Darius now reports them as having been restored according to his decree.

THEY BUILT, etc., IN THE TIME OF CYRUS, AND DARIUS, AND ARTAXERXES. This refers not to the building of the temple itself, that is, of the Holy Place and the Holy of Holies — for this was completed in the sixth year of Darius, at verse 15 — but to the completion of the courts and ornaments of the temple, and to the prosperous state of the offerings, gifts, and celebrations of the temple: for these were completed under Artaxerxes. Again, the fabric of the temple was completed under Darius, but that of the city of Jerusalem under Artaxerxes. You ask: Who was this Artaxerxes, of whom mention is here made?

Here [Artaxerxes is mentioned], for this was the son of Darius Hystaspes, who succeeded him in the kingdom. Nor does the reason appear why Ezra omitted Xerxes, and by a leap passed from Darius the grandfather to his grandson Artaxerxes Longimanus; especially since this Xerxes was most celebrated, inasmuch as he led eleven hundred thousand soldiers into battle against the Greeks, and with his forces laid waste the lands, and with his fleets the seas, and was therefore first the terror of Greece, but soon its laughingstock, when defeated he was forced to flee almost alone. Whence Daniel described Xerxes specifically, ch. 11, v. 1. Add to this: Xerxes was most favorably disposed toward the Jews, as Josephus relates, lib. 11, Antiq., ch. 5, wherefore it seems he himself promoted the building of the Temple. Finally, Ezra, already plainly aged and old, does not seem to have been fit after the times of Xerxes for so arduous a mission to Jerusalem.

Some scholars think Artaxerxes here is Xerxes, who died not long after the reformation of the commonwealth, and therefore this book of the deeds of Esdras closes with this reformation. But others, more probably, judge this Artaxerxes to be Longimanus, the son of Xerxes: first, because it is not Xerxes but Longimanus who is properly called Artaxerxes by all historians; second, the same is clear from the continuity of this history with the history of Nehemiah, which was formerly one book with this book of Esdras. For he who is called here
CHAPTER SEVEN. SYNOPSIS: Artaxerxes sends Ezra to Jerusalem, to visit, instruct, guide and govern the Jews.

Now after these things, in the reign of Artaxerxes king of Persia, Ezra the son of Seraiah, the son of Azariah, the son of Hilkiah... — He who sent Ezra to Jerusalem was Artaxerxes; likewise Nehemiah c.2 v.5 says that the same king sent Nehemiah in the twentieth year of his reign; and he who sent Nehemiah was Longimanus [Artaxerxes I], as will be clear there. The reason why Ezra omits Xerxes is that he was wholly occupied with the Greek war and gave no thought to the Jews and the Temple, nor contributed any assistance to them for this purpose. Moreover, Josephus confuses Xerxes with Artaxerxes, for he assigns 28 years to Xerxes, whereas it is established that he reigned only twenty years; hence the remaining 8 years must be assigned to Artaxerxes. Concerning the name Artaxerxes I spoke at c.2 v.6; to which add that Artaxerxes in Hebrew and Chaldee is called Artachshasta, which in Hebrew is the same as "I boil with joy," says Pagninus, from ratach, meaning to boil and bubble, and sas meaning to rejoice. Or it is the same as "hastening to place a name," derived from chars, that is "to hasten," and nach, that is "to place." Finally, the reason why Artaxerxes was so benevolent toward the Jews was Esther, whom many hold to have been his wife; for this reason Haman was hanged by him and Mordecai exalted. But this I shall examine in the preface to Esther.
MONTH OF ADAR. — This is the twelfth and last month of the Hebrews, corresponding partly to our February and partly to March.

WHICH IS THE SIXTH YEAR OF KING DARIUS. — Therefore in the sixth year of Darius the interior part of the Temple, which was properly called the Temple, into which only priests had the right of entry, was completed; but in the ninth year of Darius all parts of the Temple, both interior and exterior — namely the courts, porticoes, and other ornaments of the Temple — were finished. So Josephus, Ribera, Pererius, and others.
AS ONE MAN, — that is, by unanimous consent, as though all had been one man, having one and the same heart and mind.
WHO HAD SEPARATED THEMSELVES FROM THE UNCLEANNESS OF THE NATIONS. — Those who, having left paganism and become proselytes, had been initiated into Judaism through circumcision.

AND HE TURNED THE HEART OF THE KING OF ASSYRIA TOWARD THEM. — This is Darius king of the Persians, who is called king of Assyria because the Assyrians, once lords and monarchs of all, having been subjugated by Cyrus along with the Babylonians, were now subject to the Persian monarchy and to Darius.
Ezra the son of Seraiah... son of Aaron the chief priest. — [Genealogy of Ezra from Aaron traced through sixteen generations.]

HE HIMSELF EZRA WENT UP FROM BABYLON, AND HE WAS A READY SCRIBE IN THE LAW OF MOSES WHICH THE LORD GOD OF ISRAEL HAD GIVEN: AND THE KING GRANTED HIM ALL HIS REQUEST, ACCORDING TO THE HAND OF THE LORD HIS GOD UPON HIM.
AND THERE WENT UP SOME OF THE CHILDREN OF ISRAEL, AND OF THE PRIESTS, AND THE LEVITES, AND THE SINGERS, AND THE PORTERS, AND THE NETHINIM, UNTO JERUSALEM, IN THE SEVENTH YEAR OF ARTAXERXES THE KING.
AND HE CAME TO JERUSALEM IN THE FIFTH MONTH, WHICH WAS IN THE SEVENTH YEAR OF THE KING.
FOR UPON THE FIRST DAY OF THE FIRST MONTH BEGAN HE TO GO UP FROM BABYLON, AND ON THE FIRST DAY OF THE FIFTH MONTH CAME HE TO JERUSALEM, ACCORDING TO THE GOOD HAND OF HIS GOD UPON HIM.
FOR EZRA HAD PREPARED HIS HEART TO SEEK THE LAW OF THE LORD, AND TO DO IT, AND TO TEACH IN ISRAEL STATUTES AND JUDGMENTS. # GEM
NOW THIS IS THE COPY OF THE LETTER THAT THE KING ARTAXERXES GAVE UNTO EZRA THE PRIEST, THE SCRIBE, EVEN A SCRIBE OF THE WORDS OF THE COMMANDMENTS OF THE LORD, AND OF HIS STATUTES TO ISRAEL.
ARTAXERXES, KING OF KINGS, UNTO EZRA THE PRIEST, A SCRIBE OF THE LAW OF THE GOD OF HEAVEN, PERFECT PEACE, AND AT SUCH A TIME.
I MAKE A DECREE, THAT ALL THEY OF THE PEOPLE OF ISRAEL, AND OF HIS PRIESTS AND LEVITES, IN MY REALM, WHICH ARE MINDED OF THEIR OWN FREE WILL TO GO UP TO JERUSALEM, GO WITH THEE.
FORASMUCH AS THOU ART SENT OF THE KING, AND OF HIS SEVEN COUNSELLORS, TO ENQUIRE CONCERNING JUDAH AND JERUSALEM, ACCORDING TO THE LAW OF THY GOD WHICH IS IN THINE HAND.
AND TO CARRY THE SILVER AND GOLD, WHICH THE KING AND HIS COUNSELLORS HAVE FREELY OFFERED UNTO THE GOD OF ISRAEL, WHOSE HABITATION IS IN JERUSALEM.

AND ALL THE SILVER AND GOLD THAT THOU CANST FIND IN ALL THE PROVINCE OF BABYLON, WITH THE FREEWILL OFFERING OF THE PEOPLE, AND OF THE PRIESTS, OFFERING WILLINGLY FOR THE HOUSE OF THEIR GOD WHICH IS IN JERUSALEM.

THAT THOU MAYEST BUY SPEEDILY WITH THIS MONEY BULLOCKS, RAMS, LAMBS, WITH THEIR MEAT OFFERINGS AND THEIR DRINK OFFERINGS, AND OFFER THEM UPON THE ALTAR OF THE HOUSE OF YOUR GOD, WHICH IS IN JERUSALEM.
AND WHATSOEVER SHALL SEEM GOOD TO THEE AND TO THY BRETHREN, TO DO WITH THE REST OF THE SILVER AND THE GOLD, THAT DO AFTER THE WILL OF YOUR GOD.
THE VESSELS ALSO THAT ARE GIVEN THEE FOR THE SERVICE OF THE HOUSE OF THY GOD, THOSE DELIVER THOU BEFORE THE GOD OF JERUSALEM.
AND WHATSOEVER MORE SHALL BE NEEDFUL FOR THE HOUSE OF THY GOD, WHICH THOU SHALT HAVE OCCASION TO BESTOW, BESTOW IT OUT OF THE KING'S TREASURE HOUSE.

AND I, EVEN I ARTAXERXES THE KING, DO MAKE A DECREE TO ALL THE TREASURERS WHICH ARE BEYOND THE RIVER, THAT WHATSOEVER EZRA THE PRIEST, THE SCRIBE OF THE LAW OF THE GOD OF HEAVEN, SHALL REQUIRE OF YOU, IT BE DONE SPEEDILY.
UNTO AN HUNDRED TALENTS OF SILVER, AND TO AN HUNDRED MEASURES OF WHEAT, AND TO AN HUNDRED BATHS OF WINE, AND TO AN HUNDRED BATHS OF OIL, AND SALT WITHOUT PRESCRIBING HOW MUCH.
WHATSOEVER IS COMMANDED BY THE GOD OF HEAVEN, LET IT BE DILIGENTLY DONE FOR THE HOUSE OF THE GOD OF HEAVEN: FOR WHY SHOULD THERE BE WRATH AGAINST THE REALM OF THE KING AND HIS SONS?

ALSO WE CERTIFY YOU, THAT TOUCHING ANY OF THE PRIESTS AND LEVITES, SINGERS, PORTERS, NETHINIMS, OR MINISTERS OF THIS HOUSE OF GOD, IT SHALL NOT BE LAWFUL TO IMPOSE TOLL, TRIBUTE, OR CUSTOM, UPON THEM.

AND THOU, EZRA, AFTER THE WISDOM OF THY GOD, THAT IS IN THINE HAND, SET MAGISTRATES AND JUDGES, WHICH MAY JUDGE ALL THE PEOPLE THAT ARE BEYOND THE RIVER, ALL SUCH AS KNOW THE LAWS OF THY GOD; AND TEACH YE THEM THAT KNOW THEM NOT.

AND WHOSOEVER WILL NOT DO THE LAW OF THY GOD, AND THE LAW OF THE KING, LET JUDGMENT BE EXECUTED SPEEDILY UPON HIM, WHETHER IT BE UNTO DEATH, OR TO BANISHMENT, OR TO CONFISCATION OF GOODS, OR TO IMPRISONMENT.
BLESSED BE THE LORD GOD OF OUR FATHERS, WHO HATH PUT SUCH A THING AS THIS IN THE KING'S HEART, TO BEAUTIFY THE HOUSE OF THE LORD WHICH IS IN JERUSALEM. # GEM
AND HE HATH EXTENDED MERCY UNTO ME BEFORE THE KING, AND HIS COUNSELLORS, AND BEFORE ALL THE KING'S MIGHTY PRINCES.

They took of their daughters for themselves and their sons as wives. Note: God had commanded in Exodus 34 and Deuteronomy 7:3 that the Jews were not to take Canaanite wives who inhabited the holy land to be occupied by the Jews, and this on account of the danger of perversion; lest, namely, they be enticed by idolatrous wives to worship idols, as Nehemiah warns in the last chapter, vv. 24 and 26. The same judgment applied to Ammonites, Moabites, Egyptians, etc.: understand this unless the wives first, having cast aside idols, converted to the true God and Judaism; for then it was lawful to take them, just as Salmon took Rahab, and Boaz took Ruth.

Lyra and the Gloss assign this as the cause of the sin: that the Jews coming to Jerusalem, seeing their wives worn thin, wrinkled and disfigured from the labor and hardships of the journey, despised them, and therefore took young, vigorous and beautiful women from neighboring nations.

The hand also of the princes and magistrates was first in this transgression — as if to say: in this act of taking Gentile wives, the princes were first, who ought to have turned others away from this crime and restrained them. For the hand is applied to a work in order to perform it; and hand joined to hand is the customary pledge of mutual faith between husband and wife.
As also this day — we see, experience and feel the plagues already described, with which God continually strikes our backs and scourges our sins.

And now for a little while grace has been shown from the Lord our God, to leave us a remnant — that is to say: something small, and likely to last, as is to be feared, only a short time, we have obtained from God by our prayer, namely that it was permitted us to return from Babylon to our homeland. Hence the Septuagint renders: "And now as a little while the Lord has been made gentle toward us." For Ezra feared lest on account of these marriages contracted with Gentiles, God, angered again, might revoke, impede their return to Jerusalem, and involve them in other miseries.

And that he would give us a nail in his holy place — read thus with the Hebrew and Septuagint, not "peace of his," as some read. The meaning is: that there be given to us a nail, that is, a small measure of firmness (for nails and pegs held together tents, beams and timbers so that they stood firm) of our habitation in Jerusalem. Hence the Septuagint for "nail" renders "firmament." So Cajetan, Vatable and others. Symbolically, "nail" signifies princes and magistrates from the Jewish people: for upon them the people depended, just as a vessel or beam hangs from a nail, as I noted at Isaiah 22:23, on those words of God concerning the High Priest Eliakim: "And I will fasten him as a nail in a sure place, and he shall be for a throne of glory to the house of his father." Ezra therefore counts it as a great benefit that Artaxerxes gave the Jews princes and magistrates from their own people, who would govern them according to the laws and ancestral institutions, and advance them in the true worship of God; for had he given Persians, they would have cared little to preserve Jewish practice.

And that he would enlighten our eyes — that is, refresh and gladden (for as darkness is the symbol and cause of sadness, so light is of joy) — "and give us a little life in our bondage" — understand: a pleasant, free and glad life; for to live in mourning and servitude is death rather than life. Add also what Xenophon reports in book 7 of the Cyropaedia, that Cyrus, when after the capture of Babylon the Persians raged against the citizens, commanded that they spare and give life to those who spoke the Syrian tongue, that is, Jewish: for God put this disposition in him as a favor to the Jews.

And to set up the desolations thereof — so he calls the ruins and desolate houses in Jerusalem, which were now being restored. "And to give us a fence" — read thus with the Roman text, not "hope" as others read — that is, protection, guardianship and security from the Samaritans and other enemies who had now under Cambyses impeded the building of the city and temple; and this was accomplished through the favor of Artaxerxes, so benevolent and mighty a monarch.

Who have filled it from mouth to mouth with their uncleanness — that is, they have filled it on every side, just as a vessel is filled from mouth to mouth, that is, from one extremity to the other with vinegar or another liquid. Sanchez adds: "Mouth," he says, denotes a measure, because every measure, especially of liquids, has an opening, which is the top of the vessel, and when there is a great abundance of some liquid, it is poured from one full vessel into another through a channel, and thence again into another, until all vessels are filled to the top. By this manner of speaking, many provinces are signified as full of Gentile abomination.
Now therefore — these are the words of God prohibiting anyone from taking a Gentile wife.

And seek not their peace or their prosperity — as if to say: Care not, love not, seek not the prosperity of neighboring Gentile nations, who enjoying peace abound in wealth and pleasures, so that on that account you desire to enter into alliances and marriages with them; but rather serve your God in your land, and seek and expect all prosperity from him. Secondly, as if to say: Be not solicitous about the peace and prosperity of neighboring Gentiles, so as to rejoice in it; and if it be taken from them, to grieve; for I do not want you to enter into friendship with them and contract marriages, but rather I desire you to be turned away from them and hostile, that you may trample them down, overthrow and cut them off.

And after all that is come upon us for our evil deeds, and for our great trespass, seeing that thou our God hast punished us less than our iniquities deserve, and hast given us such deliverance as this — that is, so great a salvation that we might not again turn back and make void thy commandments, nor join in marriage with the peoples of these abominations.

Shouldest thou not be angry with us till thou hadst consumed us, so that there should be no remnant nor escaping? — Ezra asks fearfully: if we have again violated your commands by contracting marriages with Gentiles, will you not be so angered as to destroy us utterly, leaving no remnant for salvation?

O Lord God of Israel, thou art righteous: for we remain yet escaped, as it is this day: behold, we are before thee in our trespasses: for we cannot stand before thee because of this. Ezra confesses that God is just, and that they cannot stand before him on account of this sin; yet he implicitly hopes for mercy, since they are not yet utterly consumed. # GEM

[Lapide on Ezra 10, preliminary] In the same chapter, v. 3: "Because they now say: There is no king over us." And in Psalm 17, the particle O/ki (Hebrew) is placed for ornament, and the Latin "quoniam" which corresponds to it has the same force as "quia" (because), at verses 31, 32, 33, 35. Therefore the sense will be: After all these things which we have endured on account of our wicked deeds, thou, our God, hast delivered us. So Sanchez.

Secondly, more forcefully, Vatablus renders "quia" as "tamen" (nevertheless), for the Hebrew "ki," meaning "because," often functions as an adversative and is taken for "sed," "tamen," "verumtamen," as in Isaiah 36: "Can the earth travail in one day? because Zion travailed and brought forth"; that is, the earth cannot bring forth in one day; nevertheless Zion can. In Psalm 36, when David had said of the just: "They shall not be confounded in the evil time," he immediately adds concerning the wicked: "Because sinners shall perish"; that is, nevertheless sinners shall perish. And in Psalm 13, when he had said of sinners: "They trembled with fear, where there was no fear," he straightway adds of the just: "Because the Lord is in the just generation"; that is, nevertheless God will be with the just and therefore they shall not fear.

So also here; whence Vatablus renders: "But after all these things came upon us because of our evil deeds, and because of our great transgressions; thou nevertheless, O our God, didst spare us, that thou mightest not press us down by our iniquities." For Ezra admires the immense clemency of God toward the Jews, that He did not abandon them in Babylon on account of such great crimes, nor utterly destroy them, but spared them; yea, even increased them with new and great gifts, namely liberty, return to their homeland, restoration of the city and the temple; so that He seems to have contended with His own benefits against their ingratitude and evil deeds. This is divine clemency surpassing all human clemency, and proper to God alone.

["Wast thou not angry with us even unto consumption, so as not to leave us remnants unto salvation?"] That is, so as not to save some few out of so many thousands of Jews, as it were remnants of the people? The answer is: By no means; for although thou couldst most justly have done this to us on account of our crimes, nevertheless thou didst not will it. For thy clemency overcame thy justice, so that thou didst save not merely a few remnants but many thousands of Jews both in Judea and in Babylon, and didst preserve them in life, who shall revive and restore the former glory and splendor of Israel and of the commonwealth and of thy Church, as from the dead, and shall display it living again and glorious.

O Lord God of Israel, thou art just: for we are left a remnant that is saved, as it is this day. That is, many of us indeed have perished on account of sins, because thou by thy just judgment didst condemn them; yet not a few have survived and been left alive to be saved: in which matter thou hast in some sort exceeded justice, so that thou art most just and art called so, in that thou didst not destroy all, as thou justly couldst have; but didst save many, tempering thy justice with great clemency; yea, thy justice allowed itself to be overcome by thy clemency, or rather itself turned into clemency, and brought it about that clemency occupied the place of justice. For this is the excess of justice, when justice yields its place to clemency, yea, transforms and converts itself into it. # GEM
Behold we are before thee in our transgression. That is, we present ourselves to thee as guilty of a most grievous transgression, namely that we have taken Gentile wives.

For there is no standing before thee because of this. That is, this cannot by any argument be denied, excused, concealed, or defended; Hebrew: "For there is no standing before thee concerning this"; but that we fall in judgment, and are judged sinners, ungrateful, and guilty of treason against the divine majesty. Wherefore, overcome with shame, we veil our face, yea, cast it upon the ground, beseeching thy grace and mercy. Such ought to be the sense, the prayer, the shame, and the contrition of penitents.

Shechaniah son of Jehiel, of the sons of Elam, responded to Ezra and said: "We have trespassed against our God, and have taken strange wives from the peoples of the land; yet now there is hope in Israel concerning this thing."

Let us make a covenant with our God to put away all the wives, and such as are born of them, according to the counsel of my lord, and of those that tremble at the commandment of our God; and let it be done according to the law. — Shechaniah proposes a solemn covenant with God to dismiss all foreign wives and their children, according to the Law of Moses, which forbade intermarriage with pagans (Deut 7:3), lest Israel be drawn into idolatry. # GEM

Arise; for this matter belongeth unto thee: we also will be with thee: be of good courage, and do it. — Shechaniah exhorts Ezra to take authority and act decisively, promising the support of the people. It belongs to the priest and scribe of the Law to judge and execute such matters.

Then Ezra arose and adjured the chief priests, the Levites, and all Israel, to do according to this word; and they swore. — Ezra, rising from his prostration, binds the leaders and all the people by oath to carry out the dismissal of foreign wives, so that the covenant might be firm and inviolable.

Ezra rose from before the house of God and went to the chamber of Johanan son of Eliashib; and when he came there, he ate no bread and drank no water, for he mourned because of the transgression of those who had returned from captivity. — Ezra continues his fast and mourning in private, not ceasing his penance until the matter was fully resolved; a model of pastoral grief for the sins of those entrusted to one's care.

And they made proclamation throughout Judah and Jerusalem unto all the children of the captivity, that they should gather themselves together unto Jerusalem. — A public summons is issued to all the returned exiles throughout Judah and Benjamin to assemble at Jerusalem within three days, indicating the urgency and gravity of the matter.

And that whosoever would not come within three days, according to the counsel of the princes and the elders, all his substance should be forfeited, and himself separated from the congregation of those that had been carried away. — The sanction is severe: loss of goods and excommunication from the community of Israel. This double penalty, temporal and spiritual, was necessary to compel obedience in so grave a matter. The excommunication here is analogous to the ecclesiastical censure known to the Church.

Then all the men of Judah and Benjamin gathered themselves together unto Jerusalem within three days. It was the ninth month, on the twentieth day of the month; and all the people sat in the street of the house of God, trembling because of this matter, and for the great rain. — The assembly takes place in the ninth month (Kislev, corresponding to November-December), in cold and heavy rain; the people sit trembling both from the cold and from fear of God's wrath on account of their sin.

And Ezra the priest stood up, and said unto them, Ye have transgressed, and have taken strange wives, to increase the trespass of Israel. — Ezra addresses the congregation directly and solemnly, naming their sin plainly: they have transgressed the Law of God and multiplied guilt upon Israel. The duty of a pastor is to name sin clearly, not to flatter.

Now therefore make confession unto the LORD God of your fathers, and do his pleasure: and separate yourselves from the peoples of the land, and from the strange wives. — Ezra prescribes threefold remedy: confession to God, doing His will, and separation from foreign wives. Confession here is public acknowledgment of sin before God, not merely private contrition. # GEM

Then all the congregation answered and said with a loud voice, As thou hast said, so must we do. — The unanimous assent of the people, expressed with a loud voice, shows that they were genuinely moved to penance and obedience. The loud voice signifies both sincerity and public commitment.

But the people are many, and it is a time of much rain, and we are not able to stand without, neither is this a work of one day or two: for we are many that have transgressed in this thing. — The people acknowledge the magnitude of the task: so many cases require orderly examination; the bad weather makes prolonged outdoor assembly impossible. They ask not to evade the matter but to handle it properly and thoroughly.

Let now our rulers of all the congregation stand, and let all them which have taken strange wives in our cities come at appointed times, and with them the elders of every city, and the judges thereof, until the fierce wrath of our God for this matter be turned from us. — A practical and orderly procedure is proposed: designated officials with local elders and judges will examine each case city by city at appointed times, until the matter is entirely resolved and God's wrath appeased.

Only Jonathan the son of Asahel and Jahzeiah the son of Tikvah were employed about this matter: and Meshullam and Shabbethai the Levite helped them. — These four men either assisted in the commission or, according to some interpreters (Josephus, Jerome), opposed the arrangement as too lenient or too harsh. The text is ambiguous; most modern and ancient commentators take them as assistants to the commission. # GEM

And the children of the captivity did so. And Ezra the priest, with certain chief of the fathers, after the house of their fathers, and all of them by their names, were separated, and sat down in the first day of the tenth month to examine the matter. — On the first day of the tenth month (Tebeth, December-January) the commission formally begins its work; the members are named and constituted, showing the process was careful and official, not arbitrary.

And they made an end with all the men that had taken strange wives by the first day of the first month. — The entire examination was completed within three months, from the first of the tenth month to the first of the first month (Nisan). The thoroughness and speed of the process reflect the seriousness with which Ezra and the leaders approached the matter.

And among the sons of the priests there were found that had taken strange wives: namely, of the sons of Jeshua the son of Jozadak, and his brethren; Maaseiah, and Eliezer, and Jarib, and Gedaliah. — The list of offenders begins with the priests, the most culpable, since they above all were bound to know and uphold the Law. That even the sons of the high-priestly family of Jeshua son of Jozadak were among the guilty shows the depth of the corruption.

And they gave their hands that they would put away their wives; and being guilty, they offered a ram of the flock for their trespass. — The guilty priests pledged by handshake to dismiss their wives and offered a ram as a guilt-offering (Lev 5:15ff.), acknowledging their transgression formally before God. Giving the hand was the ancient sign of solemn pledge and covenant.
And of the sons of Immer; Hanani, and Zebadiah. — Two of the priestly family of Immer (cf. Ezr 2:37) are named among those who had married foreign women.

And of the sons of Harim; Maaseiah, and Elijah, and Shemaiah, and Jehiel, and Uzziah. — Five priests of the family of Harim are listed. The repeated naming of priestly offenders underscores the gravity: those who should have been guardians of Israel's purity had themselves violated it.

And of the sons of Pashhur; Elioenai, Maaseiah, Ishmael, Nethanel, Jozabad, and Elasah. — Six priests of the family of Pashhur are named. The total number of guilty priests is considerable, indicating the problem was widespread even in the sacerdotal order.

Also of the Levites; Jozabad, and Shimei, and Kelaiah (the same is Kelita), Pethahiah, Judah, and Eliezer. — Six Levites are named. That Kelaiah is identified as Kelita shows the scrupulous accuracy of the record. The Levites, as ministers of the sanctuary, shared in the priestly obligation of separateness.

Of the singers also; Eliashib: and of the porters; Shallum, and Telem, and Uri. — Among the temple singers one is named, and among the gatekeepers three. Even these humbler temple servants had contracted unlawful marriages, showing the universality of the failing.

Moreover of Israel: of the sons of Parosh; Ramiah, and Jeziah, and Malchiah, and Miamin, and Eleazar, and Malchijah, and Benaiah. — The list now turns to the lay Israelites, beginning with the family of Parosh (cf. Ezr 2:3). Seven men are named from this family alone, indicating it was numerous and prominent among the returned exiles.

And of the sons of Elam; Mattaniah, Zechariah, and Jehiel, and Abdi, and Jeremoth, and Elijah. — Six men of the family of Elam had married foreign wives. This is the same family whose representative Shechaniah had spoken first in proposing the divorce of alien wives (v. 2), showing that the reformers were not exempt from the problem themselves.

And of the sons of Zattu; Elioenai, Eliashib, Mattaniah, and Jeremoth, and Zabad, and Aziza. — Six men of the family of Zattu are named. The enumeration continues with characteristic Hebrew thoroughness, ensuring public accountability for all offenders.

Of the sons also of Bebai; Jehohanan, Hananiah, Zabbai, and Athlai. — Four men of the family of Bebai. The listing by families served the double purpose of public record and deterrence against future transgression.
And of the sons of Bani; Meshullam, Malluch, and Adaiah, Jashub, and Sheal, and Ramoth. — Six men of the family of Bani are named among those who had taken foreign wives.

And of the sons of Pahath-moab; Adna, and Chelal, Benaiah, Maaseiah, Mattaniah, Bezalel, and Binnui, and Manasseh. — Eight men of the family of Pahath-moab, one of the largest families among the returned exiles (cf. Ezr 2:6), had contracted unlawful marriages.
And of the sons of Harim; Eliezer, Ishijah, Malchijah, Shemaiah, Shimeon, — Five men of the lay family of Harim (distinct from the priestly family of the same name) are listed.
Benjamin, Malluch, and Shemariah. — Three more from the sons of Harim complete that family's list. The text continues the enumeration without pause.

Of the sons of Hashum; Mattenai, Mattattah, Zabad, Eliphelet, Jeremai, Manasseh, and Shimei. — Seven men of the family of Hashum had married foreign women. This family had returned in notable numbers (Ezr 2:19).

Of the sons of Bani; Maadai, Amram, and Uel, — Three men of another Bani family begin the next entry; the same family name appears repeatedly in these lists, suggesting several distinct clans bore the name.
Benaiah, Bedeiah, Chelluh, — The enumeration of the sons of Bani continues with three further names.
Vaniah, Meremoth, Eliashib, — Three more names from the sons of Bani.
Mattaniah, Mattenai, and Jaasai, — Three further names complete part of the Bani list.
And Bani, and Binnui, Shimei, — The Bani list continues; some scholars take these as sub-heads of the same extended family.
And Shelemiah, and Nathan, and Adaiah, — Three more names from the sons of Bani or related families.
Machnadebai, Shashai, Sharai, — Three further names are recorded in the continuing list.
