Azareel, and Shelemiah, Shemariah, — Three more offenders are named.
Shallum, Amariah, and Joseph. — Three final names close this portion of the list.

Of the sons of Nebo; Jeiel, Mattithiah, Zabad, Zebina, Jadau, and Joel, Benaiah. — Seven men of the family of Nebo conclude the list of lay offenders. The book of Ezra ends with this catalogue, which, though seemingly dry, serves as a solemn public record of penitence and reform.

All these had taken strange wives: and some of them had wives by whom they had children. — This final verse summarizes the entire list: all those named had married foreign women, and some had already had children by them. The dismissal of wives and children was a painful but necessary remedy for so grave a transgression of the covenant. The Law's requirements took precedence over natural affection, as the common good of Israel and fidelity to God's covenant demanded. # GEM

Thus, while Ezra was praying and imploring (God's mercy), and weeping, and lying prostrate before the temple. Ezra did this so that he might move the people to repentance for the long-established abuse of taking foreign wives, impelling them by his prayer and action; for he confessed here a sin not his own but the people's, taking upon himself their person and sin (just as Christ did, of whom Ezra here is a type), and therefore he instituted public prayer, with groaning and tears, prostrating himself on the ground and lying before the temple, as if considering himself unworthy to enter the temple and pray there; and praying and weeping before the temple, as the Septuagint has it, he might be heard by all the people gathered around.

And now if there is repentance in Israel concerning this. Hebrew and Septuagint: "If there is hope," namely of appeasing God through repentance. For repentance is sorrow for sins together with hope of obtaining pardon. For he who has no hope of pardon does not repent but despairs, after the manner of Judas. Wherefore repentance includes hope of remission of sin.

Let us make a covenant with the Lord our God, to put away all the foreign wives, and those who have been born of them — lest the innate love of children move us to recall their alien mothers. Yet since these are our children, it would be impious and inhuman to cast them off entirely; therefore let us cast them off not absolutely and completely, but — "according to the will of the Lord, and the precept of those who fear the Lord" — so that we follow the manner of dismissal which wise men and those who fear God shall prescribe for us.

And they swore — namely that they would absolutely and entirely dismiss the foreign wives; but concerning the sons begotten of them, not absolutely, but according to the counsel of Ezra and the wise men who fear God, they would send them away. The wise men prescribed this manner: that they should send away the sons from themselves and their households to colleges and pedagogical institutions established and to be established for this purpose, so that in these they might be instructed by prudent men in the true faith, fear, and worship of God, and in good morals. So Cajetan.

Great was this resolution of the Jews, a truly sincere repentance, and a very arduous oath. For they swore to put away their wives, to whom they had been bound with great ardor for so many years, and whom they loved as their own flesh, in order to satisfy God's will and law. The same they would do concerning their children, whom as their own limbs, indeed as living images of themselves, and as other selves — since in them they were to survive after death and live continually in the memory of men — they would cast off and as it were cut off the hope of posterity; that is to say: We prefer to please God rather than our wives and children; we prefer the grace of God to that of our wives. For we love God above all that is lovable; therefore, to obey His love, religion, and law, we will disown those beloved to us by God, and as it were cut off our own members from ourselves. We will imitate our father Abraham, who was willing to sacrifice his only Isaac to God.

Note: Some, with Bellarmine, lib. 1 de Matrimonio, c. 23, hold that these marriages of the Jews with foreigners were invalid and null, because the law of Exodus 34 and Deuteronomy 7, forbidding them, seems equally to invalidate and annul them. More probably Cajetan and others hold that by that law they were merely forbidden, not also made void. For the law expresses nothing by which it signifies them to be null. Nevertheless Ezra dissolved them, because by the law marriages could be dissolved among the Jews by the bill of divorce permitted to them, and accordingly in this case they ought to be dissolved, inasmuch as they had been contracted contrary to law and were forbidden on account of the danger of idolatry.

And Ezra rose from before the house of God, and went to the chamber of Johanan the son of Eliashib. This Eliashib was the High Priest, son of Joakim. For such was the succession of High Priests of that age: after Seraiah slain by the Chaldeans, his son Jozadak succeeded; to him Jesus, to him Joakim, to him Eliashib, as is clear from Nehemiah ch. 12, v. 22. It seems therefore that Joakim had already died, so that Eliashib succeeded him, as asserted here.
[On the twentieth year of Artaxerxes, Eliashib held the pontificate, as is clear from Neh. 3:1.]

And a voice of Ezra was sent throughout Judah — that is, a herald was sent who by his voice would summon and convoke all the Jews to Jerusalem under penalty of excommunication and confiscation of goods, that they might hear the mandate of Ezra and the princes concerning the dismissal of foreign wives. Furthermore, by Judah and Benjamin I understand the other Israelites who had joined themselves to the tribe of Judah under the leadership of Zerubbabel and the High Priest Joshua.

NOW THIS IS THE NINTH MONTH, ON THE TWENTIETH DAY OF THE MONTH. Note: The Hebrews used not solar months as we do, but lunar months. For one lunation, that is, one course of the moon through the Zodiac, constituted one month. Moreover, the first month was that whose new moon was nearest to the vernal equinox, which now falls around the 21st of March; for at that time the Hebrews were liberated from Egypt by the first celebration of the Passover and the Paschal Lamb in the first month. This first month is called Nisan, which corresponded partly to our March, partly to our April. See what was said on Exodus 12. Therefore the ninth month, of which this passage speaks, was called Casleu, which corresponded partly to our November, partly to our December. For the order of the Hebrew months is this: Nisan, Iyar, Sivan, Tammuz, Ab, Elul, Tishri, Marcheshvan, Casleu, Tevet, Shevat, Adar. So Genebrard in his Calendar of the Hebrews.

AND ALL THE PEOPLE SAT IN THE OPEN PLACE OF THE HOUSE OF GOD, TREMBLING BECAUSE OF THE SIN AND BECAUSE OF THE RAIN. — that is, they trembled inwardly from conscience, stirred by the awareness of their crime, namely that they had taken foreign wives against the law of God; and they trembled equally outwardly on account of the rains and cold, for it was November.

AND NOW MAKE CONFESSION TO THE LORD GOD. Hebrew: Give todah, that is, confession, praise, glory. — that is, repent and confess to the Lord the guilt of your sin, with a serious and effectual purpose of satisfaction and amendment, namely the repudiation of foreign wives. For this confession and repentance of yours will greatly praise and glorify God. For first it will testify that He Himself is just and you are sinners; second, it will subject you to God and to God's will and law; third, it will remove from you the danger of idolatry; fourth, it will separate you, as the faithful and holy, from contact with and contamination by unbelievers and the unclean. See what was said on Joshua 7:19, where Joshua says to Achan: "Son, give glory to God." # GEM

LET PRINCES BE APPOINTED — who shall carry out the edict now issued, and take care that each individual who has taken a foreign wife shall put her away. From this it appears that this edict was committed to execution, and that individuals, partly willingly, partly compelled by the princes, dismissed their foreign wives; for Josephus expressly narrates that this was done, in Book 11.
hoc Ciijel. aiique Esdram non praevaluisse, ut id posset exsequi ob multitudinem resistentium, qui uxoribus suis agglutinati erant, sed id per-

And there were completed [that is, fully and completely numbered, noted, and reduced to a catalogue] ALL THE MEN WHO HAD TAKEN FOREIGN WIVES. Hence their names are hereafter reviewed at length, even those of the priests; which circumstance indicates how great was the corruption of morals at that time, that it had pervaded even the priests. Moreover, just as the people, so too [the priests consented to the repudiation of foreign wives].

On the passage from Nehemiah (13:23-24) concerning wives of Ashdod, Ammon, and Moab, whose children spoke half in the language of Ashdod and knew not how to speak in the Jews' language but spoke according to the language of each people: that passage does not refute the present account. For it can be said, and indeed it is highly probable, that the Jews, after so many promises, oaths, tears, and groans, returned to their vomit, and recalled the same foreign wives or took others of a like kind. For in this place the whole people expressly consented to Ezra's repudiation of the foreign women, as is clear from verse 12, where it is said: "And all the multitude of the people answered and said with a loud voice: According to thy word unto us, so let it be done."

All these had taken foreign wives, and there were among them women who had borne sons. For "had borne," the Hebrew is *yassimu*, that is, "had placed/set"; which some explain thus: certain foreign women placed their sons, whom they had conceived by Jewish men, before the judgment and will of the judges, so that they might either cast them out or retain them. But this matter did not pertain to the women, but to the husbands and to the judges, who here dealt with the men, not with the wives, in the matter and the cause. Therefore, for women "to place" sons is the same as "to bear" sons, as our Vulgate, the Septuagint, Vatablus, and others render it.
